metadata.title: The One Thing: Your Defensible Differentiator
metadata.playlist: Say What It Is — The Conductor Brief
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 5 brief — The Conductor Brief: Part 6 (16 pts)

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 6 of 7. Part 6 is worth 16 points — tied for the most of any section. It is in two parts. Part 6A is a competitive analysis of three direct competitors, each with a sourced account of what they do well, a specific gap for your user, and evidence you actually looked at them. Part 6B is one defensible differentiator in a specific formula. Together they complete the logic that started in Part 1: your Part 1 sentence says what your tool does; your Part 6 says why no one else does it quite that way for your specific user. The reason it is worth 16 points is that this is where most tools collapse under pressure. 'We use AI' is not a differentiator. 'We are cheaper' is not a differentiator. 'We are easier to use' is not a differentiator. Those are all things a competitor can match by next week. A defensible differentiator is something structural — rooted in your specific user, your specific data, or your specific combination of capabilities.

props.segment: The One Thing: Your Defensible Differentiator
props.command: Write 3 sourced competitor analyses and one differentiator claim in the formula — the one thing no direct competitor does quite the same way for your specific user.
props.runningText: 6A + 6B together are 16 pts — this is where tools either stand on their own or collapse under pressure…
output: 6A: 3 competitors — what they do well · gap for your user · source (tried it / demo / G2)
Weak one-thing tells: AI / cheaper / easier / a feature list

beats.narration_text: Part 6A asks for three competitors. Each entry has three fields: what they do well — honest, specific, not a strawman; the specific gap for your user — not a generic weakness, but the thing they do not do for the exact person you named in Part 2A; and a source. That source is what separates a real competitor analysis from a made-up one. The accepted sources are: you tried the product — describe what you actually did and what you observed. You watched the demo — describe what the demo showed and what it did not show. You read G2 reviews or Product Hunt reviews — cite the specific pattern across multiple reviews, not a single quote. 'No sources cited' is a named pitfall in the brief and costs points. An analysis without a source is speculation. Your user's gap must be specific to the person you named. Not 'it does not do real-time processing' — that is a technical limitation. 'For an email marketing coordinator running a Monday morning report, the tool requires a 20-minute manual export step that has to happen Sunday night' — that is a gap specific to the user.

artifactLines: Field 1 — What they do well: honest and specific. Not a strawman designed to make you look better.
Field 2 — Gap for your user: specific to the Part 2A person. Not a generic technical limitation.
Field 3 — Source: tried it / watched the demo / G2 or Product Hunt reviews. No source = speculation.
G2/Product Hunt: cite a pattern across multiple reviews — not a single cherry-picked quote.
'No sources cited' is a named pitfall in the brief. It costs points.
User gap example: 'For an email coordinator running a Monday report, requires a 20-min manual export Sunday night.'
props.sparkLine: No source = speculation. Cite what you actually saw.

beats.act: finding the gap all three share
beats.narration_text: After you write the three competitor analyses, look for the gap that all three share. This is the structural gap — not the thing one competitor missed, but the thing none of them address for your specific user. That shared gap is the location of your differentiator. How to find it: put your three 'gap for your user' sentences side by side. What do they have in common? They might all require a technical skill your user does not have. They might all require a data source your user does not control. They might all produce output designed for a different role. They might all assume a workflow your user's company does not run. The shared pattern is your differentiator territory. If the three gaps are completely unrelated, you may have picked competitors that are not actually direct competitors — go back and check that all three are tools your user would consider as alternatives to yours. The gap only makes sense if the competitors are real alternatives.

props.artifactHeading: The thing all three competitors miss for your user
artifactLines: Put your three 'gap for your user' sentences side by side. What is the pattern?
Do all three require a technical skill your user doesn't have?
Do all three assume a workflow your user's company doesn't run?
Do all three produce output designed for a different role than Part 2A?
The shared pattern = your differentiator territory.
If the three gaps are unrelated, check: are these real alternatives the Part 2A user would actually consider?
props.sparkLine: The shared gap across 3 competitors is where your differentiator lives.

beats.narration_text: Part 6B is one sentence in the formula: 'The only [category] that [specific capability] for [specific user] without [the barrier competitors impose].' Four slots. Let me work through each. Category is the class of tools your product competes in — not a feature, not a technology, but the use-case category. 'The only email briefing tool' not 'the only AI-powered platform.' Specific capability is the one thing you do that fills the shared gap. Not a feature list. One capability. Specific user is the exact person from Part 2A. Without the barrier competitors impose is the differentiator — what the user does not have to do with your tool that they have to do with everyone else. This is where it must be defensible: the barrier must be structural, not cosmetic. 'Without a 20-minute Sunday-night export step' is structural — it is a workflow requirement baked into how competitors are built. 'Without complicated settings' is cosmetic — a competitor can ship a settings cleanup in a sprint.

artifactLines: Category: the use-case class. 'The only email briefing tool' — not 'the only AI-powered platform.'
Specific capability: one thing that fills the shared gap. Not a feature list.
Specific user: the exact person from Part 2A.
Without the barrier: what the user doesn't have to do with your tool that they must do with competitors.
Defensible = structural barrier. 'Without a 20-min manual export' is structural. 'Without complicated settings' is cosmetic.
Cosmetic barriers can be matched in a sprint. Structural ones require a different product architecture.
props.sparkLine: The barrier must be structural — a competitor can't match it in a sprint.

beats.act: the weak one-thing tells + defensibility test
beats.narration_text: Four tells that mark a differentiator as weak — the same discipline as the Part 1 sentence tells. Tell one: 'We use AI.' Every competitor uses AI. Using AI is a technology, not a differentiator. Tell two: 'We are cheaper.' Price is easily matched and cannot be copyrighted. Tell three: 'We are easier to use.' Easier is subjective and every competitor says it. Tell four: a feature list. 'We summarize, analyze, and visualize your data.' That is three features, not one differentiator. The defensibility test is one question: could a direct competitor copy this claim in a week? If yes, it is not defensible — rewrite. The test forces you to name exactly what is structural about your advantage. If the only thing that makes it true is a UX decision or a price point, a competitor can match it by next week. If it depends on a specific data source you control, or a specific combination of steps that requires your architecture, or a specific user workflow that only your tool supports — that is defensible.

artifactLines: Tell 1: 'We use AI.' Every competitor uses AI. Technology ≠ differentiator.
Tell 2: 'We are cheaper.' Price is easily matched.
Tell 3: 'We are easier to use.' Subjective. Every competitor says this.
Tell 4: feature list. 'We summarize, analyze, and visualize.' Three features ≠ one differentiator.
Defensibility test: could a direct competitor copy this claim in a week? If yes, rewrite.
Defensible = specific data source you control · specific architecture · workflow only your tool supports.
props.sparkLine: Could a competitor copy this in a week? If yes, it's not defensible.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Part 6. Part 6A is three competitor analyses, each with: what they do well — honest, not a strawman; the specific gap for your Part 2A user — not a generic technical weakness; and a source — tried it, watched the demo, or G2 or Product Hunt reviews with a named pattern. No sources cited costs points. Part 6B is one sentence in the formula: 'The only [category] that [capability] for [specific user] without [barrier competitors impose].' The barrier must be structural — a competitor cannot match it in a sprint. Weak one-thing tells: we use AI, we are cheaper, we are easier, a feature list. Defensibility test: could a direct competitor copy this claim in a week? If yes, rewrite. This is your A6 positioning sentence and your pitch intro.

props.artifactTitle: The One Thing: Your Defensible Differentiator
artifactLines: 6A: 3 competitors — what they do well · gap for Part 2A user · source (tried it / demo / G2).
'No sources cited' is a named pitfall. It costs points.
6B formula: 'The only [category] that [capability] for [user] without [barrier].'
Barrier must be structural — not cosmetic. Can a competitor match it in a sprint? If yes, rewrite.
Weak tells: 'we use AI' · 'cheaper' · 'easier' · feature list — all matchable in a week.
This becomes your A6 positioning sentence and your pitch intro.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. This prompt is in two parts. Part one — competitor analysis. Paste this into Claude: Here is my tool: [paste your Part 1 sentence]. Here is my Part 2A user: [paste it]. Identify three direct competitors — tools this user would consider as alternatives to mine. For each: describe what they do well, name the specific gap for my Part 2A user, and tell me where I should look to verify this — a free trial, a product demo, G2 reviews, or Product Hunt. After that runs, go and actually look at the sources it recommends. Do not submit sources you did not check. Part two — the differentiator. Paste this: Here are the three gaps I found in my competitor analysis: [paste them]. Write one sentence in the formula: 'The only [category] that [capability] for [user] without [barrier].' Then apply the defensibility test: could a direct competitor copy this claim in a week? If yes, rewrite the sentence until the answer is no. Do both passes. Do not submit a differentiator you have not checked against the defensibility test.

props.segment: The One Thing: Your Defensible Differentiator
props.command: Run two prompts. First: identify 3 direct competitors for [Part 1 sentence + Part 2A user] — what they do well, the gap for my user, and where to verify. Second: given these 3 gaps [paste them], write 'The only [category] that [capability] for [user] without [barrier].' Apply defensibility test: could a competitor copy this in a week? If yes, rewrite until the answer is no.
props.runningText: run the competitor pass first, then actually check the sources before running the differentiator pass…

beats.narration_text: The One Thing: Your Defensible Differentiator. Liam, in for Bear.
